text: Following the induction of general anaesthesia, a dual lumen endotracheal tube is sighted and the patient is positioned in the left lateral position with the right side facing up.
Standard 3 ports vats is established and a wound retractor is used in the utility incision.
The upper lobe lesion is first identified by palpation and then marked lightly with a diathermy.
The posterior lesion is then palpated and marked in a similar fashion.
Here you can see the instrument for holding the laser fibre in the VATS setting.
The laser fibre is mounted firmly within the tube, giving precise control at the tip.
The colour and intensity of the laser light at the tip can be customised to personal preference.
The laser is activated using a foot pedal and the distance between the laser tip and the lung tissue can be varied to control the intensity of the beam.
You can see that the laser is extremely precise and dissection is developed in a circumferential manner, taking care to ensure a 5mm margin is respected around the whole lesion.
Thermal extension of the laser into the tissue is approximately 5mm, giving an effective resection margin of 1cm.
The wavelength of the laser is then modified to allow coagulation of the nodule bed.
Our attention is then turned to the lower lobe lesion and the laser blender is switched back to vaporise.
The same technique is used to resect the lower lobe lesion, which is almost twice the size of its upper lobe counterpart.
Extra care should be taken when lasering a thin piece of tissue in close proximity to the chest wall, since inadvertent thermal injury can exacerbate post-operative pain.
The second specimen is removed, and a final cycle of coagulation is applied to the nodule bed.

A thorough washout with warm water is performed, and then local anaesthetic, typically 40 mL of 0.25% bupivacaine, is instilled directly in the intercostal spaces, using a hypodermic needle mounted on a Robert's forceps.
Any ooze can be controlled with light pressure from a peanut swab.
Our practice is to over-sew the nodule resection beds using a 3O PDS suture in two layers.
This may help increase pneumostasis and haemostasis.
On inspection of the lower lobe nodule resection bed, there was an area of tissue which was concerning for residual tumour.
Therefore, before over-sewing, the laser was again used to resect this suspicious piece of tissue.

To overcome challenging angles and positions, the laser can also be used through incisions other than the utility, as can be seen here when delivering a coagulation cycle to the rather posterior lower lobe resection bed.
A second sequence of oversewing with a dual layer of PDS is performed for the lower lobe resection bed.
Some additional warm water is used to test for pneumostasis, as any significant air leak should be repaired at this stage.
A chest tube is sighted apically and full lung expansion is ensured at the end of the case.
